comment_text.sn35.238:1.1: “Viper” is āsīvisā, “venom-fang”. | Uggateja (“awesome might”) is elsewhere used of the sun (SN 2.10:5.2) or the power of wisdom (Thag 19.1:5.1).
comment_text.sn35.238:10.2: Naṁ (“this”) refers back to the village; cf. SN 35.243:5.2.

root_text.sn35.238:1.2: Atha puriso āgaccheyya jīvitukāmo amaritukāmo sukhakāmo dukkhappaṭikūlo.
root_text.sn35.238:1.4: ‘ime te, ambho purisa, cattāro āsīvisā uggatejā ghoravisā kālena kālaṁ vuṭṭhāpetabbā, kālena kālaṁ nhāpetabbā, kālena kālaṁ bhojetabbā, kālena kālaṁ saṁvesetabbā.
root_text.sn35.238:1.5: Yadā ca kho te, ambho purisa, imesaṁ catunnaṁ āsīvisānaṁ uggatejānaṁ ghoravisānaṁ aññataro vā aññataro vā kuppissati, tato tvaṁ, ambho purisa, maraṇaṁ vā nigacchasi, maraṇamattaṁ vā dukkhaṁ.
root_text.sn35.238:1.6: Yaṁ te, ambho purisa, karaṇīyaṁ taṁ karohī’ti.
root_text.sn35.238:2.1: Atha kho so, bhikkhave, puriso bhīto catunnaṁ āsīvisānaṁ uggatejānaṁ ghoravisānaṁ yena vā tena vā palāyetha.
root_text.sn35.238:2.3: ‘ime kho, ambho purisa, pañca vadhakā paccatthikā piṭṭhito piṭṭhito anubandhā, yattheva naṁ passissāma tattheva jīvitā voropessāmāti.
root_text.sn35.238:2.4: Yaṁ te, ambho purisa, karaṇīyaṁ taṁ karohī’ti.
root_text.sn35.238:3.1: Atha kho so, bhikkhave, puriso bhīto catunnaṁ āsīvisānaṁ uggatejānaṁ ghoravisānaṁ, bhīto pañcannaṁ vadhakānaṁ paccatthikānaṁ yena vā tena vā palāyetha.
root_text.sn35.238:3.3: ‘ayaṁ te, ambho purisa, chaṭṭho antaracaro vadhako ukkhittāsiko piṭṭhito piṭṭhito anubandho yattheva naṁ passissāmi tattheva siro pātessāmīti.
root_text.sn35.238:3.4: Yaṁ te, ambho purisa, karaṇīyaṁ taṁ karohī’ti.
root_text.sn35.238:4.1: Atha kho so, bhikkhave, puriso bhīto catunnaṁ āsīvisānaṁ uggatejānaṁ ghoravisānaṁ, bhīto pañcannaṁ vadhakānaṁ paccatthikānaṁ, bhīto chaṭṭhassa antaracarassa vadhakassa ukkhittāsikassa yena vā tena vā palāyetha.
root_text.sn35.238:4.3: Yaññadeva gharaṁ paviseyya rittakaññeva paviseyya tucchakaññeva paviseyya suññakaññeva paviseyya.
root_text.sn35.238:4.4: Yaññadeva bhājanaṁ parimaseyya rittakaññeva parimaseyya tucchakaññeva parimaseyya suññakaññeva parimaseyya.
root_text.sn35.238:4.6: ‘idāni, ambho purisa, imaṁ suññaṁ gāmaṁ corā gāmaghātakā pavisanti.
root_text.sn35.238:4.7: Yaṁ te, ambho purisa, karaṇīyaṁ taṁ karohī’ti.
root_text.sn35.238:5.1: Atha kho so, bhikkhave, puriso bhīto catunnaṁ āsīvisānaṁ uggatejānaṁ ghoravisānaṁ, bhīto pañcannaṁ vadhakānaṁ paccatthikānaṁ, bhīto chaṭṭhassa antaracarassa vadhakassa ukkhittāsikassa, bhīto corānaṁ gāmaghātakānaṁ yena vā tena vā palāyetha.
root_text.sn35.238:5.2: So passeyya mahantaṁ udakaṇṇavaṁ orimaṁ tīraṁ sāsaṅkaṁ sappaṭibhayaṁ, pārimaṁ tīraṁ khemaṁ appaṭibhayaṁ.
root_text.sn35.238:5.3: Na cassa nāvā santāraṇī uttarasetu vā apārā pāraṁ gamanāya.
root_text.sn35.238:5.4: Atha kho, bhikkhave, tassa purisassa evamassa:
root_text.sn35.238:5.5: ‘ayaṁ kho mahāudakaṇṇavo orimaṁ tīraṁ sāsaṅkaṁ sappaṭibhayaṁ, pārimaṁ tīraṁ khemaṁ appaṭibhayaṁ, natthi ca nāvā santāraṇī uttarasetu vā apārā pāraṁ gamanāya.
root_text.sn35.238:5.6: Yannūnāhaṁ tiṇakaṭṭhasākhāpalāsaṁ saṅkaḍḍhitvā kullaṁ bandhitvā taṁ kullaṁ nissāya hatthehi ca pādehi ca vāyamamāno sotthinā pāraṁ gaccheyyan’ti.
root_text.sn35.238:6.1: Atha kho so, bhikkhave, puriso tiṇakaṭṭhasākhāpalāsaṁ saṅkaḍḍhitvā kullaṁ bandhitvā taṁ kullaṁ nissāya hatthehi ca pādehi ca vāyamamāno sotthinā pāraṁ gaccheyya, tiṇṇo pāraṅgato thale tiṭṭhati brāhmaṇo.
root_text.sn35.238:7.1: Upamā kho myāyaṁ, bhikkhave, katā atthassa viññāpanāya.
root_text.sn35.238:7.3: cattāro āsīvisā uggatejā ghoravisāti kho, bhikkhave, catunnetaṁ mahābhūtānaṁ adhivacanaṁ—
root_text.sn35.238:8.1: Pañca vadhakā paccatthikāti kho, bhikkhave, pañcannetaṁ upādānakkhandhānaṁ adhivacanaṁ, seyyathidaṁ—
root_text.sn35.238:9.1: Chaṭṭho antaracaro vadhako ukkhittāsikoti kho, bhikkhave, nandīrāgassetaṁ adhivacanaṁ.
root_text.sn35.238:10.1: Suñño gāmoti kho, bhikkhave, channetaṁ ajjhattikānaṁ āyatanānaṁ adhivacanaṁ.
root_text.sn35.238:10.2: Cakkhuto cepi naṁ, bhikkhave, paṇḍito byatto medhāvī upaparikkhati rittakaññeva khāyati, tucchakaññeva khāyati, suññakaññeva khāyati …pe…
root_text.sn35.238:10.4: manato cepi naṁ, bhikkhave, paṇḍito byatto medhāvī upaparikkhati rittakaññeva khāyati, tucchakaññeva khāyati, suññakaññeva khāyati.
root_text.sn35.238:11.1: Corā gāmaghātakāti kho, bhikkhave, channetaṁ bāhirānaṁ āyatanānaṁ adhivacanaṁ.
root_text.sn35.238:12.1: Mahā udakaṇṇavoti kho, bhikkhave, catunnetaṁ oghānaṁ adhivacanaṁ—
root_text.sn35.238:13.1: Orimaṁ tīraṁ sāsaṅkaṁ sappaṭibhayanti kho, bhikkhave, sakkāyassetaṁ adhivacanaṁ.
root_text.sn35.238:14.1: Pārimaṁ tīraṁ khemaṁ appaṭibhayanti kho, bhikkhave, nibbānassetaṁ adhivacanaṁ.
root_text.sn35.238:15.1: Kullanti kho, bhikkhave, ariyassetaṁ aṭṭhaṅgikassa maggassa adhivacanaṁ, seyyathidaṁ—
root_text.sn35.238:16.1: Tassa hatthehi ca pādehi ca vāyāmoti kho, bhikkhave, vīriyārambhassetaṁ adhivacanaṁ.
root_text.sn35.238:17.1: Tiṇṇo pāraṅgato thale tiṭṭhati brāhmaṇoti kho, bhikkhave, arahato etaṁ adhivacanan”ti.

translation_text.sn35.238:1.1: “Mendicants, suppose there were four venomous vipers of awesome might.
translation_text.sn35.238:1.2: Then a person would come along who wants to live and doesn’t want to die, who wants to be happy and recoils from pain.
translation_text.sn35.238:1.4: ‘My friend, here are four venomous vipers of awesome might. They must be periodically picked up, washed, fed, and put to sleep.
translation_text.sn35.238:1.5: But when one or other of these four venomous vipers gets angry with you, you’ll meet with death or deadly pain.
translation_text.sn35.238:1.6: So then, my friend, do what has to be done.’
translation_text.sn35.238:2.1: Then that man, terrified of those four venomous vipers, would flee this way or that.
translation_text.sn35.238:2.3: ‘My friend, there are five deadly enemies chasing you, thinking: “When we catch sight of him, we’ll murder him right there!”
translation_text.sn35.238:2.4: So then, my friend, do what has to be done.’
translation_text.sn35.238:3.1: Then that man, terrified of those four venomous vipers and those five deadly enemies, would flee this way or that.
translation_text.sn35.238:3.3: ‘My friend, there’s a sixth hidden killer chasing you with a drawn sword, thinking: “When I catch sight of him, I’ll chop off his head right there!”
translation_text.sn35.238:3.4: So then, my friend, do what has to be done.’
translation_text.sn35.238:4.1: Then that man, terrified of those four venomous vipers and those five deadly enemies and the hidden killer, would flee this way or that.
translation_text.sn35.238:4.3: But whatever house he enters is vacant, deserted, and empty.
translation_text.sn35.238:4.4: And whatever vessel he touches is vacant, hollow, and empty.
translation_text.sn35.238:4.6: ‘My friend, there are bandits who raid villages, and they’re striking now.
translation_text.sn35.238:4.7: So then, my friend, do what has to be done.’
translation_text.sn35.238:5.1: Then that man, terrified of those four venomous vipers and those five deadly enemies and the hidden killer and the bandits, would flee this way or that.
translation_text.sn35.238:5.2: He’d see a large deluge, whose near shore is dubious and perilous, while the far shore is a sanctuary free of peril.
translation_text.sn35.238:5.3: But there’s no ferryboat or bridge for crossing over.
translation_text.sn35.238:5.6: ‘Why don’t I gather grass, sticks, branches, and leaves and make a raft? Riding on the raft, and paddling with my hands and feet, I can safely reach the far shore.’
translation_text.sn35.238:6.1: And so that man did exactly that. Having crossed over and gone to the far shore, the brahmin stands on solid ground.
translation_text.sn35.238:7.1: I’ve made up this simile to make a point.
translation_text.sn35.238:7.3: ‘Four venomous vipers of awesome might’ is a term for the four principal states:
translation_text.sn35.238:7.4: the elements of earth, water, fire, and air.
translation_text.sn35.238:8.1: ‘Five deadly enemies’ is a term for the five grasping aggregates, that is:
translation_text.sn35.238:8.2: form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness.
translation_text.sn35.238:9.1: ‘The sixth hidden killer with a drawn sword’ is a term for relishing and greed.
translation_text.sn35.238:10.1: ‘Empty village’ is a term for the six interior sense fields.
translation_text.sn35.238:10.2: If an astute, competent, clever person investigates this in relation to the eye, it appears vacant, hollow, and empty.
translation_text.sn35.238:10.3: If an astute, competent, clever person investigates this in relation to the ear … nose … tongue … body …
translation_text.sn35.238:10.4: mind, it appears vacant, hollow, and empty.
translation_text.sn35.238:11.1: ‘Bandits who raid villages’ is a term for the six exterior sense fields.
translation_text.sn35.238:11.2: The eye is struck by both agreeable and disagreeable sights.
translation_text.sn35.238:11.7: mind is struck by both agreeable and disagreeable ideas.
translation_text.sn35.238:12.1: ‘Large deluge’ is a term for the four floods:
translation_text.sn35.238:12.2: the floods of sensual pleasures, desire to be reborn, views, and ignorance.
translation_text.sn35.238:13.1: ‘The near shore that’s dubious and perilous’ is a term for substantial reality.
translation_text.sn35.238:14.1: ‘The far shore, a sanctuary free of peril’ is a term for extinguishment.
translation_text.sn35.238:15.1: ‘The raft’ is a term for the noble eightfold path, that is:
translation_text.sn35.238:15.2: right view, right thought, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion.
translation_text.sn35.238:16.1: ‘Paddling with hands and feet’ is a term for rousing energy.
translation_text.sn35.238:17.1: ‘Crossed over, gone to the far shore, the brahmin stands on solid ground’ is a term for a perfected one.”

variant_text.sn35.238:5.5: natthi ca → natthassa (sya-all, km); na cassa (mr)
